description:
keyring-3.6.3/src/keyutils.rs:263 calls `KeyRing::get_persistent(KeyRingIdentifier::Session).ok()`. The `.ok()` discards any error and stores `None` in `KeyutilsCredential.persistent`. Then in `set_secret` (keyutils.rs:160-163) and `get_secret` (keyutils.rs:194-197), persistent linking is gated behind `if let Some(keyring) = self.persistent`. This means: in environments where the persistent keyring is not available (CONFIG_PERSISTENT_KEYRINGS disabled, certain container/sandbox/seccomp profiles, restricted UID namespaces, some systemd hardening configurations), the secret is stored ONLY on the session keyring with no error, no warning, and no diagnostic.

Trigger: container/CI runner with `/proc/keys` restricted, or kernel built without CONFIG_PERSISTENT_KEYRINGS. Bad behavior: secret survives only the running process's session and is gone after the process exits — far more aggressive expiration than the documented 'a few days'. The user has no way to detect this without external tooling (`keyctl show @us`).

The keyring backend (secretx-keyring/src/lib.rs:118-132 and 159-172) does not surface this distinction. There is no probe at construction (`from_uri`) or first use that detects whether the persistent keyring was acquired. Even `SecretError::Unavailable` is reserved for full storage-access failure (`NoStorageAccess`); silent persistent-keyring fallback to session-only is not modeled.

This compounds finding #1 (the docs already overstate persistence). Even if docs are fixed to say 'persists across logouts within a window', that claim is also false in restricted environments.
title: Persistent keyring may silently be unavailable; user gets session-keyring-only with no warning
